properties.name: SEA-ME-WE 5 (Marseille-Mumbai)
properties.wounding_strategy: The Digital Financial Gateway: As nations rapidly digitize their financial ecosystems, they become hyper-reliant on real-time server handshakes across continents. A coordinated 'grey-zone' disruption in the highly congested Mediterranean or Arabian Sea segments of this route wouldn't kill anyone, but it would freeze international digital payments, paralyzing gig economies and stock markets for hours or days.

properties.name: MAREA Cable
properties.wounding_strategy: High-capacity transatlantic link. Crucial for massive data replication across the Atlantic corridor.

properties.name: APG (Asia Pacific Gateway)
properties.wounding_strategy: Connecting major Asian tech hubs — Taiwan, Japan, South Korea, Singapore. Disruption severs semiconductor industry data flows and financial clearing for Asia-Pacific markets.

properties.name: Porthcurno Landing (UK)
properties.wounding_strategy: The pattern here is plausible deniability. A nation doesn't bomb these landing stations. Instead, a 'rogue' commercial fishing trawler drops its anchor over a critical transatlantic fiber-optic bundle, dragging and snapping it. The ensuing internet outage paralyzes banking in London for days. It is an economic wound disguised as a maritime accident.

properties.name: Fortaleza Subsea Hub (Brazil)
properties.wounding_strategy: Gateway between South America and Europe/Africa. Anchor-drag sabotage could paralyze Brazil's digital economy and isolate South American financial markets from global clearing systems.

properties.name: PLCN Landing (Taiwan)
properties.wounding_strategy: Cyber Severance: The microchips may be printed in Taipei, but if the data flow is severed at the landing nodes, the global supply chain halts just the same, but with minimal geopolitical fallout.

properties.name: AAE-1 (Persian Gulf Segment)
properties.wounding_strategy: IRAN CABLE THREAT: The AAE-1 cable passes through the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman — waters where Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy (IRGCN) operates with impunity. Iranian officials have explicitly threatened to cut undersea cables as retaliation for sanctions or military action. The IRGCN operates submarines and underwater drones capable of cable-cutting operations. Severing this cable would disconnect India, Pakistan, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, and East Africa from Europe simultaneously — a digital siege with no missile fired.
properties.incident_history: 2024: Iranian-backed Houthi forces cut three Red Sea cables (EIG, SEACOM, AAE-1) during Yemen conflict — first confirmed cable warfare in modern conflict. Caused ~25% reduction in bandwidth for Africa-Europe routes.

properties.name: EIG (Europe India Gateway) — Red Sea
properties.wounding_strategy: IRAN PROXY THREAT — RED SEA CHOKE: Iran's Houthi proxy cut the EIG cable in the Red Sea in early 2024 as part of their campaign against shipping. This represents Iran's doctrine of 'cable warfare by proxy' — using non-state actors to attack digital infrastructure with plausible deniability. The Bab-el-Mandeb strait is a natural chokepoint where Iranian-aligned forces can reach cables with minimal naval assets. Full severance would cripple internet access for 17 African nations and slow 15% of global internet traffic.
properties.incident_history: Confirmed cut: Feb 2024 by Houthi forces (IRGC-directed) in the Red Sea. One of three simultaneous cable attacks.

properties.name: Strait of Hormuz Crossing (FALCON/FLAG)
properties.wounding_strategy: HORMUZ DIGITAL CHOKEPOINT: Multiple cables cross the Strait of Hormuz — the same 34km passage Iran has repeatedly threatened to close to oil tankers. Iranian underwater drones (Qaher-313 class) could sever cables in this passage in under 2 hours. A single operation here would disconnect the entire Arabian Peninsula (UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman) from global internet simultaneously. Combined with oil supply disruption, this represents a dual-domain economic warfare capability. Iran has positioned this as a deterrent against US military action.
properties.strategic_note: Only 34km wide. Iran controls both shores. World's highest cable density per km of narrow waterway.

properties.name: SEACOM (East Africa Hub)
properties.wounding_strategy: SEACOM was cut simultaneously with EIG and AAE-1 in Feb 2024 by Houthi forces. East Africa's most critical internet backbone — carries 80% of internet traffic for Kenya, Tanzania, Mozambique, South Africa. Iran's proxy strategy targets cables connecting African nations that support Western sanctions policy. Cable restoration took 8 weeks, during which East African stock exchanges and banking systems operated on degraded backup capacity.
properties.incident_history: Confirmed cut: Feb 2024. Restoration completed April 2024. Estimated economic damage: $40M+ to East African economies.
